name: Whites City
short_description: BLM public land rockhounding site in the Guadalupe foothills offering quartz, agate, and jasper collecting. Personal use free collecting with 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Whites City sits on BLM land in Eddy County near the Guadalupe Mountains, an area rich in Paleozoic limestone and Cenozoic igneous activity. The terrain around Whites City features exposed rocky slopes and desert wash systems that erode and expose mineral specimens. Collectors can search the hillsides and arroyos for quartz crystals, agate nodules, and jasper fragments weathered from the surrounding formations.

The Guadalupe region's geology reflects millions of years of uplift and erosion. Silica-rich groundwater percolating through limestone and dolomite created the quartz veins and agate deposits found here. The area's accessible terrain and variety of microcrystalline quartz makes it suitable for collectors of all experience levels.

Visitors should expect desert conditions with sparse vegetation, rocky footing, and afternoon heat. The collecting area is relatively close to civilization yet retains a quiet, exploratory character. Most productive spots are within easy walking distance of parking pullouts along the access roads.
history: The Whites City area lies within the Guadalupe Mountains geological province, where Permian-age marine limestone was deposited in an ancient sea and later uplifted during Laramide orogeny. Cenozoic silica-rich fluids migrated through fractures and cavities, precipitating quartz, agate, and chalcedony that collectors find today in weathered exposures and erosional features.
tips: Search the base and banks of desert washes and arroyos where water erosion has exposed nodules and fragments. Look for translucent or banded quartz and agate specimens ranging from pea-sized fragments to fist-sized nodules. Use a geological hammer to test loose rocks in hillside talus, and examine the soil near exposed limestone outcrops where weathering concentrates specimens. Focus on north-facing slopes in morning hours to avoid intense afternoon heat and maximize visibility of light-colored quartz against darker soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 3,100 feet. Gently rolling desert terrain with sparse creosote and yucca vegetation. Rocky, uneven footing throughout the collecting area. Washes and arroyos offer easier walking but present loose rock hazards. Most productive areas are within 0.5 to 2 miles of parking pullouts. Terrain is walkable but requires sturdy footwear and careful footing on steep slopes.
best_season: October - April. This timing avoids the intense summer heat (May-September regularly exceeds 95 degrees Fahrenheit) and provides comfortable collecting conditions. Winter months are generally mild in this elevation band with occasional freezing nights but daytime temperatures typically 50-70 degrees Fahrenheit.
written_directions: From Carlsbad, take US 62/180 south toward Whites City for approximately 18 miles. The BLM collecting area begins near the Whites City community along access roads branching east and west from the highway. Look for pull-off areas and dirt roads marked with BLM signage. Specific collecting zones are accessible via rough dirt roads requiring passenger vehicles to drive slowly; high-clearance or 4-wheel-drive vehicles recommended for more remote pullouts. The main collecting area is scattered across multiple small parking zones rather than a single centralized location.
road_conditions: Mostly dirt roads and pullouts. Main highway access is paved (US 62/180). Secondary access roads are unpaved, rough, and may develop washboard. High-clearance recommended for remote pullouts. Passable year-round but may become muddy or temporarily impassable after heavy rain.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas scattered throughout the BLM land. No developed parking lot or facilities. Parking is pull-off style along access roads. Capacity varies by location but generally accommodates 2-5 vehicles per pullout. No amenities or signage at most parking areas.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. Respect all marked private property boundaries. Leave collected areas clean and do not damage surrounding vegetation or rock formations.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized excavation
No heavy equipment
Respect private property boundaries
Leave no trash
nearest_services: Carlsbad, approximately 18 miles north. Full amenities including gas, restaurants, lodging, and grocery stores. White's City community has a small visitor facility but limited services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to rolling terrain with rocky, uneven footing throughout. No wheelchair access to productive collecting areas. Most walking is moderate distance (less than 2 miles round trip) from parking. Steep hillsides available but not required for productive collecting. Sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. Manageable for families with children ages 6+ in good physical condition.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding at Whites City New Mexico?
faq.answer: Whites City BLM land is known for quartz crystals, agate nodules, banded chalcedony, and jasper fragments. Specimens range from small fragments to fist-sized nodules. The area's geology produces quality collectible quartz in translucent to milky varieties and colorful agate with banding patterns typical of the Guadalupe region.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Whites City free or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Whites City is completely free on BLM public land. No permit is required for personal use collecting. The only regulations are the 25 lb per day limit per FLPMA and the requirement that collection be for personal use only, not commercial resale.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding near Whites City?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal for rockhounding at Whites City. Summer temperatures often exceed 95 degrees Fahrenheit making afternoon collecting uncomfortable. Fall and winter provide mild daytime temperatures (50-70 degrees) and minimal rainfall, with the added benefit of clearer visibility and less vegetation obscuring the ground.

faq.question: Can you bring kids rockhounding at Whites City New Mexico?
faq.answer: Yes, Whites City is family-friendly and beginner-suitable for children ages 6 and up. The terrain is relatively easy to moderate with manageable hiking distances under 2 miles round trip. Kids can actively participate in searching rocky slopes and washes for agate and quartz specimens. Always supervise children, bring plenty of water, and use sun protection.

faq.question: How far is Whites City from Carlsbad and what gear do you need?
faq.answer: Whites City is approximately 18 miles south of Carlsbad via US 62/180. Essential gear includes a 5-gallon bucket or backpack, geological hammer or pry bar, sturdy hiking boots, sun protection (hat and sunscreen), and 2-3 liters of water per person. A magnifying glass helps identify translucent quartz and agate specimens in the field.

faq.question: Are there hazards or safety concerns rockhounding at Whites City?
faq.answer: Main hazards include intense desert sun and heat (always bring water), loose rocky footing on hillsides, sharp material when breaking specimens, and wildlife including rattlesnakes (watch where you step and place hands). Cell service is spotty so inform someone of your location. The area is relatively accessible and not extremely remote, making it safer than backcountry sites.

meta_title: Whites City Rockhounding - Free BLM Quartz & Agate
